oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Helping Carnaby’s Cockatoos
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Waugh, David
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 49 Number 2 (July 2022).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.For their stunning visual appeal, people like protea flowers. Carnaby’s Cockatoos (Zanda latirostris) like them too, but for the much more basic motive of eating them. Near the south coast of Western Australia, an area recognized as a global biodiversity hotspot, expansive areas of heathland rich in native proteaceous plants have in the past been cleared by people to make way for other uses, thus depriving not just the Carnaby's Cockatoos of important foraging areas, but also impacting many other species adapted to this spe-The Carnaby’s Cockatoo is restricted to the south-west corner of Australia, and has suffered decades of habitat loss vital for breeding and feeding, so much so that it is now classified as an endangered species. However, there are actions being taken to halt, and even reverse the trend so that these black cockatoos can be made much safer. One of the projects, managed by Australian NGO Bush Heritage and supported by the Loro Parque Fundación, is the restoration of the protaceous-rich heathland by widescale planting of appropriate native species in its 2,128 hectare Monjebup Reserve in far southwest Australia.Over the last ten years, Bush Heritage has re-established heathland on previously cleared areas through direct-seeding and seedling planting, and now flocks of dozens of Carnaby’s Cockatoos can be observed feeding there. In 2021 the project has been building on this proven success by improving 400 hectares of existingClick the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 49 No. 2 (2022): Watchbird July 2022; 24-25
